steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The second team's hypothesis is that intermediate-headed fish will be more likely than wide-headed fish to detect large-vortex obstructions in low-visibility conditions — meaning intermediate-headed fish should bump into those obstructions less often. A supporting finding must directly compare both head types in exactly those conditions (large vortices, low visibility) and show intermediate heads outperforming wide heads.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — wide-headed fish bumped into more than half of obstructions: Reports only the wide-headed fish's collision rate with no comparison to intermediate-headed fish. Without that comparison, the hypothesis (about relative performance) is not supported. ✗
  • Choice B — some intermediate-headed fish bumped into obstructions more often than others of the same species: Compares individual specimens within the intermediate-headed type, not between head types. This says nothing about how intermediate heads perform versus wide heads. ✗
  • Choice C — wide-headed fish bumped into obstructions more often than intermediate-headed fish did: Directly compares both head types in the specified conditions (large vortices, low visibility) and shows wide heads collided more — exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — intermediate-headed fish bumped into obstructions just as often as wide-headed fish: Shows equal performance between the two types, which contradicts the hypothesis that intermediate heads would outperform wide heads in these conditions. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
